evidence_text: The study dataset contains 33,596 agent-authored pull requests across five coding agents.
source_excerpt: “Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex... Copilot and Devin follow... while Cursor contributes 1,541, and Claude Code represents the smallest share with 459 PRs.”
note: This is the reported dataset size for the analyzed PR corpus.
caveat: This is the reported dataset size for the analyzed PR corpus.

evidence_text: Across all agents, 24,014 of 33,596 PRs were merged, for a reported merge rate of 71.48%.
source_excerpt: “Across all agents, 71.48% of PRs (24,014) are successfully merged.”
note: This is the aggregate merge outcome across the full dataset.
caveat: This is the aggregate merge outcome across the full dataset.

evidence_text: OpenAI Codex accounts for 21,799 PRs, the largest PR volume among the five agents in the dataset.
source_excerpt: “Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex (21,799), exceeding the output of any other agent by more than a factor of four.”
note: This evidence is about contribution volume, not merge quality by itself.
caveat: This evidence is about contribution volume, not merge quality by itself.

evidence_text: OpenAI Codex has the highest reported merge rate in the dataset at 82.59% (18,004 merged PRs).
source_excerpt: “Despite handling the largest volume of contributions, OpenAI Codex achieves the highest merge rate, with 82.59% (18,004) of its PRs being accepted.”
note: This evidence is descriptive of observed outcomes by agent.
caveat: This evidence is descriptive of observed outcomes by agent.

evidence_text: Copilot has the lowest reported merge rate among the five agents at 43.04% (2,139 merged PRs).
source_excerpt: “Copilot exhibits the lowest merge rate, with only 43.04% (2,139) of its PRs being merged.”
note: This evidence compares observed agent-level merge outcomes.
caveat: This evidence compares observed agent-level merge outcomes.

evidence_text: Documentation tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 84%, one of the highest task-type success rates across agents.
source_excerpt: “Across agents, tasks with consistently higher merge rates include documentations (84%), CI (79%), and build (74%).”
note: The source reports this as an across-agents task-type rate.
caveat: The source reports this as an across-agents task-type rate.

evidence_text: CI tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 79%, placing them among the highest-success task types.
source_excerpt: “Across agents, tasks with consistently higher merge rates include documentations (84%), CI (79%), and build (74%).”
note: The source groups CI with other comparatively high-merge maintenance-oriented tasks.
caveat: The source groups CI with other comparatively high-merge maintenance-oriented tasks.

evidence_text: Build tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 74% across agents.
source_excerpt: “Across agents, tasks with consistently higher merge rates include documentations (84%), CI (79%), and build (74%).”
note: This evidence is task-type specific and distinct from documentation and CI rates.
caveat: This evidence is task-type specific and distinct from documentation and CI rates.

evidence_text: Performance tasks have the lowest reported overall merge rate across agents at 55%.
source_excerpt: “In contrast, performance (55%) and fix (64%) display the lowest merge rates overall.”
note: This evidence is limited to observed merge-rate differences by task type.
caveat: This evidence is limited to observed merge-rate differences by task type.

evidence_text: Fix tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 64%, placing them among the lowest-merge task types.
source_excerpt: “In contrast, performance (55%) and fix (64%) display the lowest merge rates overall.”
note: This evidence is separate from the lower performance-task merge rate.
caveat: This evidence is separate from the lower performance-task merge rate.

evidence_text: Not-merged PRs show larger total lines-of-code changes than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta difference of 17%.
source_excerpt: “Based on Cliff’s δ (Table 1), the difference in total lines of code changes is 17%... both indicating a small-to-medium effect size that not-merged PRs skew toward larger changes.”
note: This is an association between PR size and merge outcome; it does not by itself establish why larger changes fail.
caveat: This is an association between PR size and merge outcome; it does not by itself establish why larger changes fail.

evidence_text: Not-merged PRs touch more files than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta difference of 10%.
source_excerpt: “Based on Cliff’s δ (Table 1), the difference in total lines of code changes is 17%, and the difference in the number of changed files is 10%... not-merged PRs skew toward larger changes.”
note: This is a reported distributional difference, not a direct causal test.
caveat: This is a reported distributional difference, not a direct causal test.

evidence_text: Not-merged PRs experience more CI failures than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta of 24%, described as a moderate effect size.
source_excerpt: “Figure 2(c) reports the distribution of CI failures for merged and not-merged agentic PRs... Cliff’s δ of 24% indicates a moderate effect size, showing that not-merged PRs tend to experience more CI failures.”
note: This evidence shows a stronger association than the size-related metrics, but still as an observed relationship.
caveat: This evidence shows a stronger association than the size-related metrics, but still as an observed relationship.

evidence_text: Each additional failed CI check is associated with about a 15% decrease in the odds of a PR being merged.
source_excerpt: “Notably, each additional failed CI check decreases the odds of a merge by about 15%.”
note: This evidence comes from the reported logistic regression interpretation.
caveat: This evidence comes from the reported logistic regression interpretation.

evidence_text: The qualitative rejection analysis began with 600 manually annotated rejected PRs, but 38 were inaccessible at analysis time, leaving 562 PRs for categorization.
source_excerpt: “We randomly select a subset of 600 rejected PRs for qualitative analysis...” and later, “We note that 38 PRs were no longer accessible at the time of analysis due to deletion or archival, leaving a total of 562 PRs for categorization.”
note: This evidence bounds the effective sample size used in the reported rejection-pattern distribution.
caveat: This evidence bounds the effective sample size used in the reported rejection-pattern distribution.

evidence_text: Reviewer-level abandonment is the most frequent rejection pattern, affecting 228 PRs, or 38% of the manually analyzed rejected PRs.
source_excerpt: “Reviewer-level abandonment is the most frequent rejection pattern, accounting for 228 PRs (38%). These PRs were left without any meaningful human reviewer interaction...”
note: This rejection pattern reflects failure before substantive review, so it limits what can be inferred about code quality from rejection alone.
caveat: This rejection pattern reflects failure before substantive review, so it limits what can be inferred about code quality from rejection alone.

evidence_text: Duplicate PRs are the most common pull-request-level rejection pattern, affecting 142 PRs, or 23% of the annotated rejected PRs.
source_excerpt: “Within this level, duplicate PRs are the most common pattern, affecting 142 PRs (23%), where maintainers explicitly reference an existing PR that already implements the same change.”
note: This evidence identifies a specific rejection pattern within the PR-level category.
caveat: This evidence identifies a specific rejection pattern within the PR-level category.

evidence_text: CI/test failure is the most common code-level rejection pattern, affecting 99 PRs, or 17% of the annotated rejected PRs.
source_excerpt: “Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex... Copilot and Devin follow... while Cursor contributes 1,541, and Claude Code represents the smallest share with 459 PRs.”
note: This is the reported dataset size for the analyzed PR corpus.
caveat: This is the reported dataset size for the analyzed PR corpus.
